attributes.stem: A patient is found to be bleeding from an ulcer in the posterior aspect of the first part of the duodenum. The ulcer has eroded into a large underlying blood vessel.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following blood vessels is most likely to be affected?
attributes.explanation:
The Gastroduodenal artery is a branch of the common hepatic artery and descends posterior to the superior portion of the duodenum before branching off into the right gastroepiploic and superior pancreaticoduodenal arteries. The position of the gastroduodenal artery relative to the proximal portion of the duodenum leaves it susceptible to transmural ulcers in this segment and can result in haemorrhage and potential hypovolaemic shock.

The arterial supply of the duodenum has an important anatomical transition point at the major duodenal papilla. Proximal to this point the supply to the duodenum is via the the gastroduodenal artery and distal to this point via the the inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery.

This transition is important – it marks the change from the embryological foregut to midgut. The veins of the duodenum follow the major arteries and drain into the hepatic portal vein.

The arterial supply to the stomach is from the branches of the coeliac trunk. The Coeliac trunk is a major abdominal artery, it arises from the anterior aspect of the abdominal aorta at the level of T12 and supplies many of the gastrointestinal viscera. The trunk itself extends only approximately 1cm before dividing into the three major branches of the left gastric, common hepatic and splenic arteries respectively. The lesser curvature of the stomach is supplied by anastomoses of the right and left gastric arteries, whereas the greater curvature is supplied by the right and left gastro-omental arteries.

The right hepatic artery is a branch of the hepatic artery proper and supplies the right lobe of the liver. The proper hepatic artery ascends through the lesser omentum in its path towards the liver.

The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) is the second of the three major anterior branches of the abdominal aorta, arising anteriorly from the abdominal aorta at the level of the L1, positioned immediately inferior to the origin of the coeliac trunk. From its origin on the abdominal aorta, the SMA descends down the posterior aspect of the abdomen with some significant anatomical relations with the pyloric part of the stomach, splenic vein and neck of the pancreas positioned anterior and the left renal vein, uncinate process of the pancreas and inferior part of the duodenum posterior to the SMA.

The venous draining of the stomach runs in parallel with the arteries, with the left and right gastric veins draining into the hepatic portal vein, as part of the portal venous system. Contrastingly, the short gastric vein, and gastro-omental veins ultimately drain into the superior mesenteric vein as part of the systemic venous system.
learningPoints: Transmural duodenal ulcers can erode into the gastroduodenal artery causing haemorrhage and potential hypovolaemic shock
references: Yule E. The Arterial Supply to the Duodenum. J Anat. 1927 Apr;61(Pt 3):344-5. PMID: 17104148; PMCID: PMC1249963.

Chaudhry SR, Liman MNP, Peterson DC. Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Stomach. [Updated 2021 Oct 14]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482334/
